title: God's Loudest Anti-Gay Voice Was Allegedly Paying a Male Escort for Three Years
teaser: Ted Haggard, president of the National Association of Evangelicals and a leading opponent of gay marriage, resigned in November 2006 after a male escort alleged Haggard had been his client for three years. The escort also alleged Haggard had purchased methamphetamine from him.
body:
There is a particular kind of institutional irony that arrives not with a whisper but with a full press release and a letter of resignation. That is precisely what arrived on November 2, 2006, when Ted Haggard, one of the most recognizable evangelical voices in the United States, stepped down from the organization he had helped define. The circumstances were not ambiguous, and they were not going away quietly. A male escort had gone to the press, and the press had obliged.

Haggard had served as president of the National Association of Evangelicals, an organization representing approximately 30 million American Christians. That is not a niche constituency. That is a theological constituency roughly the size of California, with opinions on policy, morality, and the divine order of marriage. Haggard had been their designated spokesman, and he had performed the role with considerable conviction.

The man who came forward was Mike Jones, a Denver-based male escort who alleged that Haggard had been his paying client for approximately three years. Jones did not describe a single lapse in judgment. He described a three-year professional arrangement. The allegations also included the claim that Haggard had purchased methamphetamine from Jones, which added a second and distinctly secular layer of contradiction to the story.

To appreciate the full geometry of this situation, one must consider where Haggard was standing when it collapsed. He had been a prominent and vocal opponent of same-sex marriage, lending his considerable platform and his organization's institutional weight to efforts to restrict marriage to heterosexual couples. His theological objections were not peripheral. They were central to his public identity.

Beyond the pulpit, Haggard had cultivated a relationship with the executive branch of the federal government that most pastors do not achieve and most lobbyists would envy. He reportedly met weekly with President George W. Bush and served as a regular faith adviser to the Bush White House. His influence was not merely congregational. It was federal.

The timing of the disclosure was worth noting. Jones came forward just days before the November 7, 2006 midterm elections, at a moment when same-sex marriage ballot measures were appearing across multiple states and evangelical voter mobilization was considered a critical component of Republican electoral strategy. The revelation landed at the intersection of theology, politics, and logistics.

New Life Church, the Colorado Springs congregation Haggard had founded, was also swept into the fallout. Haggard stepped down from leadership there as well, recognizing, or being advised, that the gap between his public position and the allegations now in print was too wide to bridge from the pulpit. The church, which had grown under his leadership into a significant evangelical institution, was left to process a founder-level credibility crisis.

The National Association of Evangelicals moved with notable speed to separate the organization from its now-former president. Institutional self-preservation operates on a faster timeline than theological deliberation, and the board did not take weeks to reach a conclusion. Haggard's resignation was accepted, and the process of organizational distancing began with the kind of efficiency that suggests the mechanism had been prepared in advance, at least as a contingency.

What makes the Haggard case analytically distinct from a routine scandal of personal failing is the scaffolding of policy and advocacy that surrounded it. A pastor who preaches fidelity and has an affair has committed a recognizable and tragically common contradiction. A pastor who spends years lobbying against the legal recognition of same-sex relationships while allegedly maintaining a paid relationship with a man for three years is operating in a different register of institutional contradiction entirely.

The policy influence Haggard wielded was not incidental to this story. He had not merely expressed personal religious beliefs from a private pulpit. He had carried those beliefs into the West Wing, into federal policy conversations, and into the public square as the representative voice of 30 million constituents who presumably believed he meant what he said.

Methamphetamine, for its part, does not appear anywhere in the evangelical policy platform as a recommended recreational substance. Its alleged presence in this narrative functions less as an addendum and more as a kind of editorial underlining. The story did not require additional complication, and yet complication arrived anyway, which is perhaps the most reliable characteristic of situations built on sustained concealment.

Accountability journalism has a professional obligation to resist the cheap satisfaction of the obvious punchline. This story provides so many that restraint becomes its own discipline. What remains after the restraint is applied is a factual record of a man who held institutional authority over the moral direction of millions of Americans, who met weekly with a sitting president to discuss values and policy, and who was simultaneously, according to his accuser, doing something else entirely on a regular basis for three years. The record stands without editorial embellishment.
subjectTitle: Former President, National Association of Evangelicals; Founder, New Life Church
